nativeRange.habitat: Randall's Assessor Basslet is found on coral reefs in the Western Pacific, typically inhabiting reef gullies at depths of roughly 1 to 6 metres. In the wild it is commonly encountered hovering near the entrances of holes, caves, and overhangs.

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer meaty marine foods such as mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped fresh or frozen marine fish, squid, copepods, and frozen carnivore preparations. In the wild, has been documented feeding on small zooplanktonic crustaceans. Feeding at least twice daily is commonly recommended. Some specimens have been reported to accept quality pellets or flakes over time.

avoidWith: fish of similar shape and territory

summary: A small, vivid blue cave-dwelling reef fish from the Western Pacific, generally considered one of the more approachable choices for beginner reef aquarists. Known for its characteristic upside-down swimming beneath ledges and overhangs.
careNotes:
Randall's Assessor Basslet (Assessor randalli) is a small, cryptic, cave-associated reef fish first described by Allen & Kuiter in 1976 and originating from the Western Pacific (Japan, Taiwan, and the Philippines). It is generally considered one of the more approachable small basslets for beginner to intermediate marine aquarists. Like its congeners, it tends to spend much of its time near caves and overhangs, often swimming upside down — a natural behavior tied to its cave-dwelling lifestyle rather than a sign of illness. Ample live rock with caves and crevices is widely regarded as essential for this species' wellbeing.

The species is generally considered peaceful toward most unrelated tankmates but tends to become territorial toward conspecific males and fish of similar shape that compete for the same cave space. It is best kept singly or as a confirmed mated pair; multiple males in the same system are generally reported to be problematic.

Reproduction: Like other Assessor species, the male is reported to perform paternal mouthbrooding, incubating the egg mass in his mouth for roughly two weeks. Captive-bred specimens have been produced and generally acclimate more readily than wild-caught fish.

Reef safety: Generally considered fully reef-safe — it does not pose a threat to corals or sessile invertebrates. Very small ornamental shrimp could theoretically be at risk given the species' carnivorous diet, but predation is not commonly reported.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooding has been documented for the genus Assessor; the male incubates the egg mass in his mouth for roughly two weeks. Captive breeding has been achieved, and captive-bred specimens are commercially available. Specific breeding-difficulty data for A. randalli is limited in the hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Plesiopidae confirmed, correcting LiveAquaria's Grammidae listing), authority (Allen & Kuiter, 1976), maximum size (4.0 cm TL), native range (Western Pacific, 28°N-30°S, 119°E-154°E), depth range (1-6 m), habitat (coral reef gullies; nervous at hole entrances), and confirmation of paternal egg-guarding behavior.

additional.name: Quality Marine

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply

additional.name: Liverocknreef
additional.notes: Confirmed Western Pacific origin (Japan, Taiwan), Blue Assessor trade name, peaceful/timid temperament, and reef-safe status. Outlier size figure (4 in) was disregarded in favor of FishBase + LiveAquaria consensus.

fish.breedingNotes: Members of the genus Assessor are paternal mouthbrooders; the male incubates the egg mass for approximately two weeks. Captive-bred A. randalli specimens (notably from ORA) have been produced. A specific difficulty rating for this species is not well established in the hobby literature.
